persona.name: Elena Vasquez

occupation.title: Park Ranger (Interpretation Division)
occupation.organization: Coconino National Forest, USDA Forest Service
occupation.description: Leads guided hikes and educational programs for visitors. Monitors trail conditions and wildlife sightings. Works outdoors 80% of the time in all weather. Seasonal schedule shifts — busier in summer, quieter in winter.

persona.education: B.S. in Environmental Science from Northern Arizona University. Wilderness First Responder certification. Completed a seasonal internship at Grand Canyon National Park that turned into a full-time position.
long_term_goals: Complete a 50-mile ultramarathon on trails she's helped maintain
Advance to a district ranger position
Reduce her environmental footprint to near-zero
Visit every national park in the western US by age 40
Start a trail running clinic for underrepresented communities
persona.style: Elena is relaxed, competent, and specific about gear. She talks about shoes the way a mechanic talks about engines — interested in how they handle specific conditions, not how they look. She's direct without being brusque, uses outdoorsy vocabulary naturally (switchbacks, scree, exposure), and has zero patience for products that prioritize aesthetics over function. She wears earth tones, Patagonia, and trail shoes that look like they've been through something.

traits: Grounded and self-reliant — used to solving problems alone on trails
Passionate about outdoor access and environmental justice
Detail-oriented about gear specs — reads reviews like field reports
Unimpressed by brand prestige; values durability and repairability
Quietly competitive in trail races but doesn't broadcast it
Uncomfortable in urban or indoor-focused environments

interests: Trail running on single-track and technical terrain
likes: Aggressive tread patterns and rock plates
Shoes that drain water quickly after creek crossings
Brands with environmental commitments (not just marketing)
Shoes that last 500+ miles on rocky terrain
dislikes: Shoes that fall apart after 200 miles
Excessive cushioning that reduces ground feel
Flashy designs meant for Instagram, not trails
Shoes that don't come in women's sizes below 7
Urban running culture that ignores trails

beliefs: Public lands belong to everyone — access is a justice issue
Gear should be bought for durability first, performance second, looks last
Trail running teaches you more about yourself than road running ever could
If a brand talks more about sustainability than it practices, that's worse than saying nothing
skills: Trail navigation using topo maps and GPS
Wilderness first aid and emergency response
Long-distance trail running in technical terrain

general: Runs trails 4 times per week — two easy, one long, one speed
Tests new shoes on her most punishing trail before committing
Keeps a gear log noting mileage, tread wear, and conditions
Buys from brands with repair/resole programs when possible

morning: Trail run from her doorstep into the forest
workday: Lead guided hike or educational program
evening: Gear maintenance — clean shoes, check tread
Read trail running reports or guidebooks
weekend: Recover with stretching and food prep
Sunday: rock climbing or mountain biking
Trail maintenance volunteer shift once a month

health.physical: Excellent cardiovascular and musculoskeletal fitness. Rolled her ankle twice in 2024 — both on loose scree, both minor. Careful about hydration in Arizona's dry climate. Slight IT band tightness she manages with foam rolling.
health.mental: Very stable. Finds peace in solitude on trails. Gets restless and irritable in cities. Low stress baseline due to outdoor lifestyle. Occasionally lonely living in a small mountain town.

relationships.name: Carlos Vasquez
relationships.description: Construction foreman in Tucson. Proud of Elena's career but doesn't understand why she runs for fun. Taught her to be self-reliant.

relationships.name: Rosa Vasquez
relationships.description: School cafeteria manager in Tucson. Sends Elena care packages with homemade tamales. Wishes she lived closer.

relationships.name: Jordan Whitehorse
relationships.description: Navajo, works in tribal land management. They share a cabin outside Flagstaff and run together on weekends.

relationships.name: Sam Oakes
relationships.description: Fellow ranger who paces her on long runs. They critique gear together over post-run coffee.
